title: TopoLM: brain-like spatio-functional organization in a topographic language model

reviews.summary: This paper introduces TopoLM, a transformer-based language model endowed with a two-dimensional topographic layout at each attention and MLP layer and trained with a spatial smoothness loss in addition to next-token prediction. The spatial loss encourages nearby units to have correlated responses, yielding emergent functional clusters that the authors show align with neuroimaging observations of the human language system (core language network selectivity, noun/verb clusters, and concreteness effects). The model attains comparable downstream task performance to a non-topographic baseline (slightly lower on BLiMP and Brain-Score, slightly higher on GLUE) while producing brain-like spatio-functional organization without fitting to neural data.
strengths: Extends the TDANN-style spatial smoothness principle—well-established in vision modeling—into the language domain, demonstrating its generality across cortical systems.
Introduces a practical spatial loss for transformers by operating on small randomly sampled neighborhoods to reduce compute while favoring local smoothness.
Maintains full attention connectivity and multi-head attention, avoiding restrictive local connectivity constraints used by prior topographic language models (Topoformer).

Clear, multi-pronged evaluation: (i) emergence of a spatially organized language network; (ii) noun/verb clustering; (iii) concreteness-specific clustering consistent with neuroimaging; (iv) fMRI-like readout sampling to bridge model-to-brain measurement.
Quantitative spatial statistics (Moran’s I) and threshold-free maps complement thresholded cluster visualizations.
Inclusion of a non-topographic control and a prior topographic LM (Topoformer-BERT) as baselines.
Model and loss are described succinctly; figures convey the spatial loss mechanism, readout simulation, and emergent cluster maps.
The neuroscientific context and hypotheses are stated clearly, with appropriate cross-referencing to empirical literature.
Provides a unified and parsimonious account that spatial smoothness can drive language-like cortical topography, paralleling similar findings in vision.
Offers an aligned, stimulus-computable tool for generating model-based hypotheses in language neuroscience with minimal cost in standard NLP performance.
weaknesses: The spatial organization is layer-specific with randomly permuted 2D maps per layer; there is no continuous “tissue” across layers, which limits mechanistic mapping to cortical continuity and perturbation predictions across depths.
The spatial loss uses Pearson correlations estimated from mini-batch activations; stability and variance properties of this estimator under typical batch/token sampling are not analyzed.

Choice of L∞ neighborhood metric and fixed α across layers is motivated but not systematically ablated; robustness to design choices (L2 vs L∞, neighborhood size/number, α schedule, layer-specific weighting) is underexplored.

Baseline comparisons are partially mismatched: Topoformer-BERT differs in objective (masked LM), corpus size, and architecture (single head), limiting the strength of conclusions drawn from this comparison.

fMRI-like smoothing strongly increases apparent clustering for both models; while the authors report unsmoothed vs smoothed results, a deeper analysis quantifying how much of the effect is due purely to sampling assumptions is warranted.
Limited ablations on the necessity of random spatial permutations per layer, the number of neighborhoods, and neighborhood radius beyond brief statements.
The “core language network” uniformity result is shown qualitatively; a more formal statistic (e.g., intraclass correlation across clusters, effect sizes) would strengthen the claim.
Details on how unit activations are aggregated across batch and sequence length when computing correlations could be more explicit (e.g., sample size for r, token vs position averaging).
The definition of “response” as mean absolute activation could be better justified relative to linear readouts commonly used in brain-model comparisons.
Beyond Topoformer, limited discussion of alternative ways to instill topology (e.g., Laplacian regularization, Gaussian smoothing penalties, self-organizing maps in language settings).
No comparison to simple post-hoc spatial smoothing baselines applied to a non-topographic model during analysis time (to test whether training-time smoothness confers unique structure).
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive are the results to the fMRI-like smoothing parameters (e.g., FWHM, assumed unit spacing)? Can you provide a sweep to show the stability of Moran’s I differences between TopoLM and the baseline?
2. Could you report quantitative statistics for the “uniform response profiles” across language-selective clusters (e.g., average pairwise correlation across clusters with confidence intervals), and compare TopoLM vs the baseline?
3. How are unit activations sampled for computing Pearson correlations in the spatial loss—across batch elements, sequence positions, or both? What is the effective sample size per neighborhood, and does it affect training stability?
4. Did you explore alternative spatial penalties (e.g., Laplacian smoothness, kernel-weighted correlation, or direct distance-weighted MSE) and/or different distance metrics (L2 vs L∞)? If so, how do topography and performance change?
5. Can you include a more systematic α sweep and neighborhood count/size ablation to chart the trade-off between topography quality (e.g., Moran’s I) and downstream/LM performance?
6. How much compute overhead does the spatial loss introduce (e.g., percentage slowdown, memory increase), and how does the neighborhood approximation scale with model width?
7. Could you provide a better-controlled baseline by re-training Topoformer-like variants (e.g., same corpus and auto-regressive objective) to isolate the effect of connectivity vs smoothness?
8. Beyond noun–verb and concreteness, did you test other syntactic/semantic features (e.g., animacy, morphological features) to assess the generality of emergent clustering?
reviews.assessment: This paper presents an elegant and timely extension of topographic inductive biases to language modeling, showing that a simple spatial smoothness loss atop a standard transformer yields brain-like spatio-functional organization with minimal performance cost. The work is conceptually significant for the NeuroAI community, building a bridge from TDANNs in vision to the language system and producing concrete, testable predictions (e.g., concreteness-dependent noun/verb clustering). While the baseline comparisons are imperfect and some analyses (ablation depth, sensitivity to smoothing, quantitative uniformity across clusters) could be strengthened, the core claims are supported by multiple convergent evaluations. I view this as a solid, intellectually meaningful contribution that will stimulate further research on spatially grounded language models and their neurobiological interpretability. I recommend acceptance, contingent on addressing the requested analyses to fortify causal interpretations and robustness.
